title: Kleedehn, Moore have early lead
summary: With the Copper Basin 300 Sled Dog Race yet to leave the Alaska road system Saturday afternoon, it was a little early to assess how the race was shaping up. The usual suspects were towards the front -- with one minor surprise. The usual suspects included 2004 Copper Basin champ William Kleedehn, 2005 Copper Basin champ Allen Moore, and a couple cagey, old veterans of this race: David Sawatzky and John Schandelmeier. Kleedehn, a one-legged dog driver from Carcross, Yukon Territory, led Moore, a Two Rivers musher married to former Yukon Quest International Sled Dog Race Champion Aily Zirkle, into the community of Chistochina. They were only minutes apart. Just behind them were Schandelmeier, who claims Maclaren River along the Denali Highway as home, and Sawatzky, a former Healy resident now…

title: Frostbite is a real danger for those venturing out
summary: D efending Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race champion Jeff King was all frosty smiles when he followed his dog team to victory in the Knik 200 last weekend. Here was a man who appeared to be clearly in his element, despite the sub-zero temperatures. Little did most race fans know, but beneath King's warm, protective mitts lurked the crippling damage of what may be Alaska's most insidious winter sports injury -- frostbite. Almost everyone who has spent much time on the winter trails of Alaska will have suffered some frostbite somewhere. Usually the tips of the nose, ears or cheeks go white, then red and finally black as the skin dies. But legendary arctic adventurer Dick Griffith got the nickname "Old Black Ass'' for another part of his anatomy that changed colors when frostbitten by viciously c…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED New Year's toast marks 368 summits of Flattop in 2006 CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: January 7, 2007 Last Modified: January 7, 2007 at 04:49 AM Click to enlarge Bob Stanton FLATTOP MOUNTAIN -- Despite a nasty wind blowing spindrift snow everywhere, there was a summit toast with canned champagne last Sunday to celebrate 63-year-old Bob Stanton's ending 2006 the way it began -- with his boots planted atop Alaska's most-climbed peak. The real story, though, was not in the climb to end the year or the climb that started the year but the climbs in between. All 366 of them, which when added to the first and last brought Stanton's total for the year to 368. That's an average of more than one Flattop summit per day for 2006 or, as noted mountain runner Brad Precosky quickly calculat…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Putman, Stassel claim Alyeska slalom races Published: January 23, 2007 Last Modified: January 23, 2007 at 04:02 AM SNOWBOARDING -- Kiana Putman, a former USA Snowboarding Association national champion, and Ryan Stassel picked up victories Sunday in the Big Alaska USASA Slalom at the Alyeska Resort. Putman posted the fastest women's time of 1:12.26, while Stassel had the fastest men's time of 1:15.14. Travis Golay, competing in the open men's division, had the best single-run time of 32.44 in the first run of his second race. But he missed a gate on the second run and had to hike up the hill, which hurt his time. Snowboard and free-ski competition continues with a slopestyle event Sunday at Alyeska in Girdwood. For complete results, visit www.bigalaskasnow.net . Big Alaska Snowboard and Fre…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Three Alaskans named to U.S. team at worlds Daily News staff Published: January 17, 2007 Last Modified: January 17, 2007 at 02:51 AM Alaska Olympians Kikkan Randall, Lars Flora and James Southam are among a dozen U.S. cross country skiers named to compete next month in the Nordic World Championships in Sapporo, Japan. The World Championships will be Feb. 22-March 4 in Sapporo, host city for the 1972 Olympic Winter Games. "This team has the experience, the capacity and the support around them to achieve a lot at these championships," said Luke Bodensteiner, U.S. nordic team director. "This team is built around veterans, who are entering their peak years and who are hungry for big results. There is not a weak link here." Andy Newell of Vermont, who produced the first U.S. top-three finish in…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: KLONDIKE 300 Qualifying races help Iditarod rookies By RON WILMOT Anchorage Daily News Published: January 21, 2007 Last Modified: January 21, 2007 at 09:56 AM The path from Iditarod dream to Iditarod reality can be a long one for sled dog mushers. There is a ton to learn -- training and caring for the dogs, learning what to pack, safely driving the sled. That learning curve only steepens when mushers go from daily or overnight training jaunts on local trails to running the 1,100-mile Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race from Anchorage to Nome. Four Iditarod rookies set out Saturday to complete the Klondike 300, a qualifying race -- Michael Suprenant of Chugiak, Sigrid Ekran of Sparbu, Norway, Tom Lesatz of Two Rivers and Hernan Maguieira of Ushuaia, Argentina. Iditarod rookies must complete two ap…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: MUSHING Smith leads Klondike ALASKA SPORTS DIGEST Published: January 21, 2007 Last Modified: January 21, 2007 at 06:02 AM Ramy Smith pulled out of the Deshka checkpoint at 5:26 p.m. Saturday, in the lead of the Klondike 300 sled dog race. Smith spent two minutes in the checkpoint, arriving and leaving only seconds ahead of Ray Redington. Both mushers sported 16-dog teams. Michelle Phillips and her 12-dog team left four minutes later in third place. Klondike 300 Time out of Deshka checkpoint Saturday's times (Musher, dogs, time) 1) Ramy Smith, 16, 5:26 p.m.; 2) Ray Redington, 16, 5:26 p.m.; 3) Michelle Phillips, 12, 5:30 p.m.; 4) Matt Hayashita, 16, 5:33 p.m.; 5) Raymie Redington, 12, 5:34 p.m.; 6) Michah Degerlund, 15, 5:35 p.m.; 7) Ryan Redington, 12, 5:39 p.m.; 8) Gabrielle Dunham, 14, 5…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CRAIG MEDRED Only here do commercial fishermen own halibut CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: January 21, 2007 Last Modified: January 21, 2007 at 04:56 AM The people who manipulated the political system to make halibut into a luxury item priced almost out of reach of the average consumer are at it again. To keep their lock on 90 percent of Alaska's flatfish catch, commercial fishing interests are now going after John Q. Angler. The International Pacific Halibut Commission last week endorsed a plea from commercial fishermen to halve the bag limit for Alaska halibut anglers to one fish per day for parts of the season, claiming anglers are threatening the resource by killing and taking home what is estimated to be an annual catch of 9 million pounds. That sure sounds like a lot of halibut steak…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: LAINE WELCH Fleet primed for profitable snow crab catch LAINE WELCH FISHERIES Published: December 16, 2006 Last Modified: December 16, 2006 at 12:23 AM KODIAK -- Bering Sea crabbers are hoping for a bigger paycheck for Alaska's largest crab fishery, with almost no snow crab inventory in the nation's freezers and crimped catch quotas in competing countries. The 2007 season will be in full swing by mid-January, and while most crab markets are pinched by oversupply, that's not the case for snow crab. "We would like to see an advance price of $1.60," said Greg White, a negotiator for the Inter-Cooperative Exchange, crab harvesters who hold about 70 percent of the Bering Sea king and snow crab quota shares. "We think that's supportable in the current market environment." The average price for A…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Snowmachiners spend New Year's stranded on Kenai hilltop SURVIVAL: They were well-equipped, but cut-off cell call prompted a rescue. By PATRICE KOHL Peninsula Clarion Published: January 14, 2007 Last Modified: January 14, 2007 at 03:33 AM KENAI -- In the final hours leading up to the new year, Matthew Offrink and Trent Larson thought they would be celebrating with friends in Ninilchik, but instead found themselves wading in waist-deep snow as they climbed a ridge in Caribou Hills to make a cell phone call letting family know they were stranded. "It was incredibly difficult," Offrink said. "I would take maybe two, three steps at most and we would stop for a second and catch our breath." On New Year's Eve, the two Anchorage men had been snowmachining in the hills when they got off course. De…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Knowing how to prevent frostbite it better than having to treat it Outside endeavors require precautions in extreme temperatures By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: January 14, 2007 Last Modified: January 14, 2007 at 05:35 AM WHAT IS FROSTBITE? • Frostbite is the freezing of tissue. Phases range from freezing a few cells on the surface of the skin to freezing cells deep in tissue. HOW DO YOU GET FROSTBITE? • Skin must be cooled to less than 24 degrees for tissue to freeze. The colder the air temperature, the quicker this can happen. Wind-chill temperatures were devised as a way to warn of frostbite danger. Wind chill doesn't make the temperature any colder, but it accelerates the rate at which tissue freezes by blowing away the warmth of body heat. HOW DO YOU AVOID FROSTBITE? •…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: COPPER BASIN 300 Steer leads Copper Basin race SNOW ISSUES: As mushers loop back to finish, weather closes in. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News Published: January 15, 2007 Last Modified: January 15, 2007 at 01:53 AM A cadre of Canadians was chasing Sheep Mountain's Zack Steer across a snowy Nelchina Basin on Sunday night as the Copper Basin 300 Sled Dog Race wound its way back toward the Glennallen start/finish line. Snow was falling lightly as the lead teams left Meier's Lake Roadhouse and headed back into the rolling hills south of the Tangle Lakes, race officials reported, but the National Weather Service was warning that could change significantly overnight. The agency posted a snow advisory through 9 a.m. today, warning of 5 to 15 inches and winds up to 25 mph. Race officials said…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: CANTWELL CLASSIC 200 Mackey wins inaugural Cantwell Classic Daily News staff Published: January 15, 2007 Last Modified: January 15, 2007 at 01:53 AM Two-time Yukon Quest champion Lance Mackey mushed a team of dogs with some bounce in their stride across the finish line at 1:50 a.m. Sunday to win the inaugural Cantwell Classic 200. "That was fun," he told the race Web site while hugging his wife Tonya and son Cane -- the fourth-place finisher in the Junior Cantwell Classic -- at the finish line. Mackey, who recently moved his training to Fairbanks, beat Hugh Neff of Skagway by 40 minutes. Former Iditarod champion Mitch Seavey was third and his son, Danny, was fourth. Melanie Gould of Talkeetna finished fifth. Cantwell Classic 200 Finish time on Sunday 1) Lance Mackey, 1:50 a.m.; 2) Hugh Nef…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: 17-year-old joins U.S. Snowboarding Team Boardercross flier gets torch from Rosey By MELISSA DeVAUGHN Anchorage Daily News Published: January 7, 2007 Last Modified: January 7, 2007 at 03:18 AM GIRDWOOD -- Callan Chythlook-Sifsof sits at an empty table at the Sitzmark Cafe, her hands resting on the table in front of her. She's wearing purple snow pants and a white coat. All around her, bustle is nonstop. Snowboarders and skiers come in from enjoying fresh snow, brushing it off their woolen hats as they step into the cafe for a warm bite to eat. Everyone is smiling. It's holiday season, a chance to relax and enjoy the moment. Callan, on the other hand, has a faraway look in her eyes, likely because she is still in shock after learning she is the newest member of the U.S. Snowboarding team. T…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Waterproof Big Lake trails map offered for sale Published: January 10, 2007 Last Modified: January 10, 2007 at 05:56 AM BIG LAKE -- A new waterproof map is available for riders planning to venture onto Big Lake-area snowmachine trails. Randi Perlman, executive director at the Big Lake Chamber of Commerce, said snowmachiner safety was the goal behind the maps. A grant from the state Division of Parks and Outdoor Recreation paid for the initial printing of about 200 maps, Perlman said. Members of the Alaska State Snowmobile Association helped with the design. The color map is printed on 8 1/2-by-11-inch waterproof paper and available for $5 from the Big Lake Chamber of Commerce, Big Lake Power Sports, Xtreme Machine, McGhan Construction or Big Lake Arctic Cat in Big Lake. The maps show parki…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Icy track promises speed at national championships RAINS: Large Alaska group starts competition today. By MATIAS SAARI Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: January 3, 2007 Last Modified: January 3, 2007 at 11:47 AM HOUGHTON, MICH. -- Coach Scott Jerome watched in horror Sunday as heavy rain soaked an already minimal snowpack just three days before this Upper Michigan town was scheduled to host the U.S. Cross Country Skiing Championships. "It was brutal. It was pouring rain," said Jerome, whose squad of 10 UAF Nanooks composes half the Fairbanks contingent at the week-long series of races. "Fortunately, the race organizers just closed the trail and let the trail sit on Monday so the water could drain out and freeze up." The rain later morphed into a bit of snow and the result is a wickedly…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Teenager stuns field at U.S. championships NORDIC SKIING: Randall records her second runner-up finish in two days of races. By MATIAS SAARI Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: January 5, 2007 Last Modified: January 5, 2007 at 02:52 AM HOUGHTON, MICH. -- Young Alexa Turzian finished her 10-kilometer freestyle race in the lead, then watched the scoreboard in disbelief as, one by one, the biggest guns in American cross-country skiing failed to beat her time. Olympians Lindsay Williams, Kikkan Randall and then Lindsey Weier, who helped tow Terzian through half the race, all crossed the line at the U.S. Cross Country Ski Championships in exhaustion -- and behind the Idaho teen. So did Elizabeth Stephen, now 19 and last year's freestyle co-champion with Anchorage's Randall. "It was nervewracki…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Little's wrong turn lets King win Knik 200 MUSHING: Kasilof musher's strong team gets tangled, loses out to the defending champion. By KEVIN KLOTT Anchorage Daily News Published: January 8, 2007 Last Modified: January 8, 2007 at 02:16 AM KNIK LAKE -- Heading north to race dogs in this weekend's Knik 200-Joe Redington Sr. Memorial Sled Dog Race was a last-second decision for Kasilof musher Jon Little. But the last decision he made Sunday morning on the Iditarod Trail, just a mile short of the finish line, cost him the 200-mile race by three minutes. One wrong turn into a dog lot owned by a former Iditarod musher tangled Little's team, squandered his two-mile lead over defending Knik 200 and Iditarod Trail Sled Dog Race champion Jeff King and shattered Little's dream of capturing the first v…

summary: Randall 2nd, Flora misses 3rd in Nordic championships By MATIAS SAARI Fairbanks Daily News-Miner Published: January 4, 2007 Last Modified: January 4, 2007 at 03:47 AM HOUGHTON, Mich. -- The U.S. Ski Team dominated the podium, but Alaskans made their mark here Wednesday in the opening races of the U.S. Cross Country Skiing Championships. Anchorage's Kikkan Randall led a dozen top-25 Alaska finishers by claiming silver in the women's 5-kilometer classic race, while a late fall by Lars Flora on the Michigan Tech Trails may have cost the Anchorage Olympian a podium spot in the men's 10-K race. The course started out fast for the women's late-morning race, but afternoon temperatures exceeded 40 degrees, turning parts of the men's track slushy and slippery, creating waxing challenges for hundred…

summary: Agreement on lagoon left no one satisfied REFORM: Parks members hope to toss Anchorage's agreement with the Owenses. By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News Published: December 19, 2006 Last Modified: December 19, 2006 at 01:18 AM Click to enlarge Some city Parks and Recreation Commission members are pushing to kill a deal the city made with private landowners who encroached on public parkland at Westchester Lagoon. The commissioners have also called for reform of the process that led to the agreement, which was reached without a public hearing. Tom and Tennys Owens, who own a home adjacent to the Tony Knowles Coastal Trail, built a rock-lined pond mostly on public property next to the trail.
